speaker: interviewer
text: Paula, while you were a commandant at Skarzysko, were you aware of any sabotage at the factories committed by the Jewish inmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember one girl. She was probably working underground with somebody. Because she took out some ammunition. And each girl was searched before going out or in or home. And I remember that they searched her. And they found some amu-- ammunition on her. And they took away. And they shot her. This was one incident I remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Otherwise, I did not hear about others. It went on, I know. So maybe somebody did that, and it wasn't noticed. But, but I heard that it went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: If this had happened in a work gang, would the commandant be punished by the Germans for this kind of activity?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: No--

speaker: interviewer
text: Given the name nature of the Nazi--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were never punished.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were never punished for what your work gang did or didn't do?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were-- the Jews were punished, not the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm talking about the Jewish commandants, like yourself. Were you punished for--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't [INAUDIBLE]--

speaker: interviewer
text: For anything the work gang did?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or didn't do?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't did sabotage. What happened didn't occur in my factory where I was working. This was on another one, where they had the stuff finished-- finished ammunition. In mine, it was prepared to do-- to be finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in that other factory, would the commandant have been punished for the sabotage that happened under her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I didn't hear about that. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of whether there was an underground in Skarzysko?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hadn't-- I didn't know. If it was, I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any attempts at escape from Skarzysko?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lots of escapes, as I said before. They brought them back, and some-- that some escaped. And some, I met here after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ammunition factory, were there mainly Jewish inmates working? Or were there any local Polish people working in the factories?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of Polish people were working, too. These were the meisters. But they worked together with the, the Jewish people. And sometimes, these Polish people helped the Jews bring in something from home, or something to eat, or they paid them. I did not know anybody. Where I worked in my part of the factory, there weren't too many Poles. And I didn't speak to them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But it happened. It happened. Lots of things happen that I did not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more about what you mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, the happenings, there was told what happened. And I was surprised. I heard that there was an underground. But I did not know who and where, when. And, and lots of people, probably-- I assume so-- took out some ammunition to help the others. But the Poles weren't very helpful, for sure, in this instant-- instant.

speaker: interviewer
text: When there was an act of sabotage or someone escaped from camp, were the other inmates punished for what anybody else did in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were very punished. Sabotage was-- when one piece of ammunition fell down on the floor, that was sabotage. All day, they were working on them. And there was some controllers. They controlled the ammunition, if it's good or if it's bad. [INAUDIBLE] it was called. If this was found, they were-- they were taken in a special room and beaten. And the commandant had to beat them. The Jew commandant had to beat the other inmates very severely. And the German just looked. And they came down. They were terrified. And they couldn't sit. They were-- that's how they were beaten. I-- they said once I should come up and see that. And I said to that commandant, I'm not going. And I can not see it. And please don't ask me to come up. And I never went up. I don't know how they did it. But the girls came down and the men-- what they were beaten. And they said what, what, what, what goes on there, it was a tragedy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the general relationship between the inmates and the commandants, generally speaking?

speaker: interviewee
text: The inmates with the commandant had to be in good, in good [INAUDIBLE]-- in good condition, I mean, to be good to each other. Because if they weren't good, they were punished. And to me, I was towards them like my-- like a sister and a brother. They had-- they depend on me like their own mother and father. They knew that what I say or what I do is for their good, not for mine. I didn't want them to be, to be punished to the Germans. So I said to them, you have to be and obey what I say. Otherwise, the German will do that. But I never talked to them rough or took a stock-- a stick-- and, and, and, and beat them. And they, they were very happy with me. They were very happy with me. It depends on the person.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain at Skarzysko?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Skarzysko, we remained to 1944. And then, then we were sent to Cze-- Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was this that you left Skarzysko?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was also around beginning of the summer-- May or June. And over there was also the same-- a ammunition fa-- factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think it was that you were transported from Skarzysko to Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: By then, we did not know. We heard something is going on. But I think the Russian, Russian came in till-- they occupied Poland till Lemberg or somewhere. And they were a clash between them. And they were afraid they were coming in right way. But they stopped them. So they transported us to Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Lemberg known by another name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lvov.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get transported from Skarzysko to Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: By trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many of them were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But quite -- because not all of them came to Czestochowa. Some of them were sent away, right away, to Germany. And some of them went to Plaszow. And some of them went to [INAUDIBLE]. Not all of them came into that camp where I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that transport take, from Skarzysko to Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few [? nights, ?] a few hours, few hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that journey like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad-- we did not know where we go. They didn't tell us where we go. But when we came, we came to the place. It wasn't as bad as in Skarzysko.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you on this transport? Were your sisters in the camp, Skarzysko, or were they with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: With me, all of them-- we were always together. But in Czestochowa, as in the other camp, there were different camps, too. There were [? Belzec, ?] where I was. And my sister and her boyfriend-- she had a boyfriend then-- they sent to Warta.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I and my youngest sister, Bella, were in [? Belzec, ?] in Czestochowa. And she was in the Warta. After the liberation, we met halfways. You mentioned that your sister had a boyfriend. How was it possible for a man and a woman to have contact in a camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they worked at the same machine. They worked at the same machines. And he fell-- she fell in love with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a commandant?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Then, she worked. After the-- when she fell in love with him, she was wor-- beginning, she was a commandant. Then she was by the machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it possible to change from the one to the other?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was possible. He probably did, did have another one on her place. And she didn't want to be. So she wanted to go to the machine to be with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way was Czestochowa different to Skarzysko?

speaker: interviewee
text: The food was a little bit better. I wasn't a commandant in there. I-- they stopped because there were different people there. There were people living there in Czestochowa already. And we were just inmates-- I and my sister, Bella, and Chajka. We came at the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: I forgot to ask you. Did you have a number at Skarzysko?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have any numbers. They didn't live through it to give us the numbers. They were close to it, but they-- the, the liberation came first.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Czestochowa--

speaker: interviewee
text: The same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you receive a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the barrack conditions like and the food conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The food conditions were a little better, because we had a piece of bread and a little jam. We didn't have that. And the soups were a little bit better. And we lived in one great hall-- about 300 women in one hall, in the same hall. And then, this was starting. A fire brea-- broke out. They send us to barracks. From-- they had barracks, too. They send us to the barracks. And there, we-- there we stayed till liberation, in the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you mainly Polish nationality, or were there other nationalities in Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mainly from Poland, mainly-- and then, from Germany were a few-- not much, but mainly from Ger-- from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there such a thing as a blockalteste in Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: A what?

speaker: interviewer
text: A blockalteste or someone in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: A blockalteste, yes-- so I was a blockalteste, too. I was on the [? battery ?], on the-- in the factory and at home in the barracks. I had to see its-- for the cleanliness, who has to do the, the, the cleaning today. I had to assign them and what to do. And when the blockfuhrer or the director came in to, to see how it is, I had to salute to them and say how many is in the barrack, and how we are, and everything should be just right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give us the names of the meister, the director, in Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czestochowa I didn't know any.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know anybody. I just went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same. I worked with the, with bullets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness any violence, murders, in Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in control of the camp there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even know who was. Must have been one commandant. And the Ger-- I know-- just know the Germans from Skarzysko. They came with us, the same, the same, the same meisters. And they knew us. So they even took-- one meister took letters from me to my sister to, to Warta because they came in and out. But I didn't know about any violence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware that the Russian front was advancing at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that stage, we heard about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's always somebody who knows everything. And we knew. And we were sitting on the, on the bunk beds and singing. One was crying, one was singing. And I always encouraged them, you'll see. We will live through it. And we'll be happy and joyful on the-- when we'll be free, we'll meet together, and we'll be friends. And, and don't worry and don't cry. Also, I didn't feel like this, but my-- I encouraged them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did this come to an end in Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 16, 1945-- it was cold. The Germans came in and said to us in the barracks, come with me to Germany. The Russians are coming. They will kill you all. And the Jews started to go with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: On a march?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the march to the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Death march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they went to Germany. And they were still there till May 5th, or whenever it-- the liberation start-- finished. And I said to my sisters, whatever will happen, let it happen here. I'm not going anywhere. Anyway, the barracks were from wood. I didn't want to stay in the barracks. I went into a [INAUDIBLE], a big build-up wash room, not from wo-- from wood. I have to-- And we sat there. Excuse me. [DRINKS] We sat there on the floor-- concrete floor, floor. And it was very big frost. We were freezing. And I'm not moving from here. And we remained till about four o'clock in the morning when started-- then the light started. Or it was still dark. Some people went out to see what's going on. And they came back right away and said, people, we are free! You can go out. The gates open-- no Germans by the gates! Let's go out! And-- but I was still afraid. I said, no, I'm not going till the light will be on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till it will be daytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was afraid to go out in the dark. It was still dark when they came back. In the daytime, we'll go out and see and know where it-- what it is. And so we did. Right away, the-- I don't know-- a committee or who-- but they made-- right away, they gave us something to eat. And they made a hot soup. But I didn't want to eat. When I went out, I saw, the like, terrible things-- dead horses, dead people, dead soldiers. And all over, revolvers and [INAUDIBLE], everything. And it was just like a battle from the history. But I was only interested to see my sister from Warta. So they came halfways. We went to Warta. And they came to [? Belzec ?]. We met halfways. And from then on, we were together. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did her boyfriend survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: With her boyfriend, with her boyfriend-- they came together. And the boyfriend came with us, just like a brother. We were together all the time. And right after we came to Lodz, the liberation, they got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to clarify something you said earlier. Are you saying that some of the camp inmates were more scared to wait for the Russians than to go with the Germans on a march?

speaker: interviewee
text: It seems so, if they went with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would you think that would be the case?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know what happened to these people. Why did they go with them? What were they afraid of the Russians? I can't understand. And a mass-- masses of people went-- a mass of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: They weren't forced to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: They were not forced?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not forced. They were not forced. I wasn't forced. Nobody was forced. They just said so. Come with me. And they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say you were scared to go out, was it fear of the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, wasn't fear of the Russians. It was just fear of the dark to go out. I didn't know Czestochowa, where to go, what, what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Paula, how did you get to Lodz? You mentioned that you and your sisters and her boyfriend-- what is his name? Her boyfriend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Buchman. Tobias Buchman.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage to get to Lodz after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we went by, by train. The train were very seldom going. But we waited for a train. And we went by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe that everything was over? And, and what did that feel like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was feeling terrible. How can I feel good with-- we didn't have anybody? We were immediately coming out. And we were orphaned. We didn't have any family-- just what I had, my sisters, that's all. And from such a large family as we were, we were-- we had nobody. Nothing on our feet-- wooden shoes we had, which were broken, and here the cold was terrible, and nothing on, on ourselves, and not the heat, and nowhere to go. It was devastating. We-- I didn't know what to tell them, my sisters. But we went to the same city where we left. To Suchedniow, we went back first to see maybe we'll get back from the Poles what we hide-- hid there before we left. But this Pole was a very nice man. He said--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Venderhovski. ?] And he said, my dear, Paula, you can't have anything to me. We were in very bad circumstances. Didn't-- we didn't have what to eat ourselves. And we sold your stuff. I can't give you back anything. I understood that. Because his brothers were officers in the Polish army. And they were shot, too. And this I know before I went-- before I went to camp. A very, very nice family-- and they helped us before the war, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I didn't do anything. What could I do? But I went to other Poles, where the neighbors told me that they took all our belongings. Go to them. So we wen. And he had a big dog. And he ch-- said dog, get the. Get them. Get them. And the j-- dog jumped on us. And we were very afraid. We ran away. We didn't even come, come to the house and to ask for anything. Because that's why he did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from then on, we left Suchedniow. And we said, we don't want to be here anymore. We want to go to a big city. So we went to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to the house where you grew up in, in Suchedniow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My landlord's son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did he respond to you? What was their reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very cold, very cold, very cold-- I, I thought they would be happy for us, because we were on such good terms with them. They were-- they didn't do anything, but they were very cold. It was very surprising to me. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did that feel like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, it felt terrible, terrible. I knew-- I grew up here with these people and everything. So I figured that I'll come back. And I-- only us in this whole family are back. Maybe they will, they will have some pity on us. And they'll give us something. They didn't even invite me to the house. I didn't go their house. They didn't give me anything. They knew in what circumstances we are. We don't have anything. So we didn't stay one more minute there. We left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Lodz. In Lodz--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were conditions like in Lodz for survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: We found a house where Germans were living there, probably. But it was empty-- or Jews, I don't know. And all of us lived together with my si-- all, all four sisters and my brother-in-law. And right away in Lodz, even, I went to work to a company who gathered [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your sister and brother-in-law get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your sister and Buchman, Tobias, get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 19th of September-- no, Oct-- September, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that was after you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: After you left Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. After they got married, I heard that my-- one of my brothers is-- lives in Germany, in Feldafing. Because he asked for us, and I asked for him. Somehow, I got to know that he's alive. So I didn't want to stay in Poland anymore. And I said to my sisters, I am going by myself. I'll have to find him. And I did found him in Feldafing.
